title: Raksha Bandhan
intro: Raksha Bandhan is one of the most cherished Hindu festivals, celebrating the sacred bond of love, trust, and protection between brothers and sisters.
description:
Raksha Bandhan is one of the most cherished Hindu festivals, celebrating the sacred bond of love, trust, and protection between brothers and sisters. The festival is observed on the Full Moon day (Purnima) of the Hindu month of Shravana, usually falling in August. On this auspicious occasion, sisters tie a sacred thread known as Rakhi around their brothers’ wrists, pray for their long life, happiness, prosperity, and good health, while brothers promise to protect and support their sisters throughout life.

According to Hindu traditions, Raksha Bandhan is associated with several inspiring legends, including the stories of Lord Krishna and Draupadi, Goddess Lakshmi and Lord Vishnu, and Indra and Indrani. These stories highlight the significance of the sacred thread as a symbol of protection, affection, faith, and lifelong commitment. Over time, Raksha Bandhan has become a celebration of family values, love, and mutual respect.

Raksha Bandhan is celebrated with great joy across India and by Hindu communities around the world. Families gather to perform traditional rituals, sisters apply Tilak, tie Rakhi, perform Aarti, and offer sweets to their brothers. The festival strengthens family bonds and promotes the values of love, gratitude, responsibility, and unity.

Festival Month

Shravana (August)

Significance / Importance

Raksha Bandhan holds great religious, cultural, and social significance in Hinduism. The festival symbolizes the eternal bond between brothers and sisters, built on love, trust, care, and responsibility. The sacred Rakhi is considered a symbol of divine protection and blessings.

The festival also teaches the importance of family unity, mutual respect, compassion, and lifelong support. Beyond biological relationships, Raksha Bandhan is celebrated among cousins, friends, and even within communities as a symbol of goodwill, protection, and harmony. It serves as a reminder to cherish relationships and uphold the values of love and responsibility.

Puja Vidhi

1. Wake up early, take a holy bath, and prepare the puja area.
2. Arrange a puja thali with Rakhi, Kumkum, Akshata (rice), a diya, sweets, and coconut.
3. Worship Lord Ganesha and the family deity.
4. Apply Tilak and Akshata on your brother’s forehead.
5. Tie the Rakhi on your brother’s right wrist.
6. Perform Aarti and offer sweets.
7. Pray for your brother’s long life, happiness, prosperity, and well-being.
8. The brother offers a gift and vows to protect and support his sister.

Related Katha / Mantra / Aarti / Chalisa

* Raksha Bandhan Katha
* Shri Krishna Mantra
* Shri Vishnu Mantra
* Shri Ganesha Mantra
* Shri Krishna Aarti
* Shri Vishnu Aarti
* Shri Ganesha Aarti

faq.a1: Raksha Bandhan is celebrated on the Full Moon day (Purnima) of the Hindu month of Shravana.
faq.a2: It celebrates the sacred bond of love, protection, trust, and lifelong support between brothers and sisters.
faq.q3: What is the significance of Rakhi?
faq.a3: Rakhi symbolizes love, protection, blessings, and the lifelong bond between siblings.
faq.q4: In which month is Raksha Bandhan celebrated?
faq.a4: Raksha Bandhan usually falls in August.
faq.q5: Which deities are associated with Raksha Bandhan?
faq.a5: The festival is associated with Lord Krishna, Lord Vishnu, Goddess Lakshmi, and Lord Ganesha through various Hindu traditions and legends.
faq.q6: What are the main rituals of Raksha Bandhan?
faq.a6: Applying Tilak, tying Rakhi, performing Aarti, offering sweets, and exchanging gifts are the main rituals.
faq.q7: Can Raksha Bandhan be celebrated beyond siblings?
faq.a7: Yes. Raksha Bandhan is also celebrated among cousins, close friends, and others as a symbol of affection, respect, and protection.
faq.q8: What is the spiritual significance of Raksha Bandhan?
faq.a8: Raksha Bandhan symbolizes love, trust, protection, family unity, and the importance of nurturing lifelong relationships with compassion and responsibility.
